text: Since 1990, the world has lost 420 million hectares of forest; an area larger than the European Union.
Tropical forests once covered 12% of Earth's land. Today they cover around 5%.
The world loses approximately 10 million hectares of forest per year; down from 16 million in 1990, but from a much smaller remaining base.
Half of all habitable land on Earth is used for agriculture; 77% of that agricultural land is dedicated to livestock.
77% of agricultural land is used for livestock; yet animal products provide only 18% of the world's calories.
Agricultural expansion drives up to 90% of global tropical deforestation.
A third of the world's agricultural land has been degraded by erosion in the past 40 years.
Three-quarters of the world's crop species depend on pollinators; insects underpin 35% of global food production by volume, including most fruits, nuts, and vegetables.
ref: FAO; Klein et al. 2007; IPBES Pollinator Assessment 2016

text: Approximately 40% of invertebrate pollinator species (including bees and butterflies) are currently at risk of extinction.
The global economic value of pollination services is estimated at $235–577 billion annually; provided almost entirely by wild and managed insects now in steep decline.
21 of the world's 37 largest aquifers are being depleted faster than they can naturally replenish.
ref: Richey et al., Water Resources Research / NASA 2015

text: Agriculture accounts for around 70% of all freshwater withdrawals globally; livestock alone accounts for roughly 30% of agriculture's total water footprint, while occupying 77% of its land.
The Ogallala Aquifer (which irrigates 30% of all US groundwater-fed agriculture) took millions of years to fill and is being drained in decades.
Monitored wildlife populations have declined by an average of 73% since 1970.
Freshwater species populations have declined by 85% on average since 1970; the sharpest collapse of any habitat type on Earth.
The last decade (2014–2023) was the hottest ever recorded.
Global sea levels have risen 21–24cm since 1880, with the rate of rise doubling since 2006.
The number of recorded climate disasters has increased fivefold over the past 50 years.
ref: WMO Atlas of Mortality and Economic Losses 2021

text: CO₂ levels in the atmosphere are now higher than at any point in at least 800,000 years.
The 2023–2025 global coral bleaching event (the fourth ever recorded and the most severe in history) impacted 84% of the world's reefs across 82 countries and territories.
ref: ICRI / NOAA Coral Reef Watch, April 2025

text: During the first global bleaching event in 1998, 21% of reefs were affected. In 2025, it was 84%.
ref: ICRI / NOAA Coral Reef Watch, April 2025

text: Around 8 million tonnes of plastic enter the world's oceans every year.
Only 9% of all plastic waste produced globally has ever been recycled.
In the United States, the plastic recycling rate is just 5%; despite decades of consumer recycling campaigns.
The world needs to produce roughly 60% more food by 2050 to feed a projected population of 9.6 billion; yet climate change is simultaneously reducing our capacity to do so.
ref: Climate Impact Lab / Stanford & Rutgers, Nature 2025

text: Every additional degree Celsius of warming reduces global food production capacity by approximately 120 calories per person per day.
ref: Climate Impact Lab / Stanford & Rutgers, Nature 2025

text: Climate change is projected to reduce global staple crop yields by 8% by 2050 regardless of future emissions; by 24% by 2100 under a high-emissions scenario.
72% of the world's staple crops (maize, wheat, soybeans, and rice) are grown in just 5 countries; a synchronized failure across all four is projected to become possible every 11 years by mid-century.
Microplastics have now been detected in human blood, lungs, placentas, and breast milk.
A 2024 study found microplastic particles in arterial plaque; patients with higher concentrations had a significantly elevated risk of heart attack and stroke.
ref: Marfella et al., New England Journal of Medicine 2024

text: The cost of solar power has fallen by 90% in the last decade; the fastest cost decline of any energy source in history.
The ozone layer is on track to fully recover by 2066, thanks to the 1987 Montreal Protocol; the most successful international environmental agreement ever reached.
Global renewable energy capacity grew by 50% in 2023; the fastest rate in 20 years.
Protected land areas have grown from 10% to 17% of Earth's land surface since 1990, with an international target of 30% by 2030.
2025 became the first full calendar year on record to exceed 1.5°C of warming above pre-industrial levels; the threshold set by the Paris Agreement.
The International Court of Justice declared in 2025 that nations have legally binding obligations to prevent climate harm; a landmark shift in international climate law.
Global negotiations for a legally binding plastics treaty collapsed in 2025, leaving no international framework to halt the 8 million tonnes of plastic entering the ocean each year.
For the first time, scientists formally attributed specific heatwave deaths to named fossil fuel companies; opening the door to direct legal liability.
ref: World Weather Attribution / Climate Attribution Science 2025

text: The UN High Seas Treaty entered ratification in 2025, aiming to protect 30% of international ocean waters by 2030; the first legal framework ever to govern the high seas, which cover nearly half the planet.
Green sea turtle nesting populations rebounded significantly in 2025; a rare conservation success story driven by decades of international protection efforts.
Atmospheric CO₂ concentrations are projected to reach levels that measurably impair human cognitive function within 50 years; levels already regularly exceeded in poorly ventilated buildings today.
